Fig. 12: Near-end (NEXT) and far-end (FEXT) crosstalk at 15 GHz for the worst-case victim TSV, comparing the proposed signal–ground assignment with the state-of-the-art configuration in [22]. Insets show the corresponding TSV layouts. Bars denote the proposed design and line markers the results reported in [22]. Fig. 1: Comparison of representative TSV modeling and optimization frameworks in terms of scalability, frequency range, and computational cost.

Fig. 6: Overview of the proposed ML-assisted optimization framework for TSV arrays. Large-scale analytical data generated from RLCG-based circuit simulations (80,000 training and 20,000 validation samples) are first used to pre-train a physicsinformed Graph Neural Network (GNN). The TSV array is represented as a fully connected graph with geometry-aware node features and distance-based edge features. The architecture integrates feature-wise linear modulation, Graph Transformer message passing, symmetry-enforced output heads, and a homoscedastic physics-aware loss function. The pre-trained model is subsequently fine-tuned using high-fidelity FEM data (10,000 samples) generated by Ansys HFSS through a simulation-tosimulation (Sim-to-Sim) transfer learning strategy.
